mnemonics.title: OS FUNCTIONS MEMORY AID
mnemonics.content: REMEMBER THE FUNCTIONS OF OS WITH: PMFMDS - P stands for PROCESS, M for MEMORY, F for FILE, M for MANAGEMENT (device), D for SECURITY, S for SHELL (user interface). OR USE: PLEASE REMEMBER MY FILE DEVICE SECURITY - PROCESS, RESOURCE MANAGEMENT, MEMORY, FILE, DEVICE, SECURITY.

mnemonics.title: BOOT PROCESS SEQUENCE
mnemonics.content: USE ORDER: P-B-L-O-S-U - POWER ON -> BIOS -> LOADER -> OS KERNEL -> SERVICES START -> USER INTERFACE. ALTERNATIVE: POST BOOTS LOADING OPERATING SYSTEM SERVICES USER.

mnemonics.title: OS TYPES MEMORY AID
mnemonics.content: REMEMBER OS TYPES WITH: BATCH RUNS JOBS, MULTI KEEPS PROGRAMS READY, TIME SHARES USER, REAL TIME NEVER DELAYS, DISTRIBUTED NETWORK UNITES, NETWORK SHARES RESOURCES.

acronyms.usage: The fundamental software that manages computer hardware and provides services to applications
Text-based interface where users type commands to interact with the OS
Visual interface with windows, icons, menus for user interaction
Set of functions and procedures that allow applications to use OS services
Firmware that initializes hardware and starts the boot process
First sector on hard disk containing boot loader and partition table
OS designed for immediate response in embedded systems
Mechanism for processes to communicate and synchronize with each other

visualTips.visualization: Think of a building with a SECURE FLOOR (kernel mode/privileged) where only authorized personnel (OS services) can enter, and a PUBLIC AREA (user mode) where regular users (applications) can work. The kernel has special powers (privileged instructions) that regular programs cannot use.
Imagine a student studying with multiple books on the desk. When stuck on one subject (waiting for I/O), they switch to another. This keeps them productive (CPU busy) rather than idle waiting.
Think of a professor taking multiple classes simultaneously by giving each group 2 minutes of attention in rotation, making each group feel they have dedicated time with the professor.
Picture a wedding cake with layers. Bottom (hardware) is the foundation, each layer above builds on the one below, and the top layer (user interface) is what users directly interact with.

analogies.concept: Operating System as a Resource Manager
analogies.analogy: Like a TRAFFIC CONTROLLER at a busy intersection managing cars (processes), allocating time slots at the signal, ensuring fair access, preventing accidents, and keeping traffic flowing smoothly.

analogies.concept: Operating System as an Extended Machine
analogies.analogy: Like a CAR MECHANIC who understands complex engine internals but provides you with a simple STEERING WHEEL and PEDALS. You drive easily without knowing engine mechanics - the mechanic (OS) handles complexity.

analogies.analization: Like the MANAGER of a hotel who works 24/7 handling critical operations (room allocation, maintenance), while guests (applications) use hotel facilities but cannot access management functions.

analogies.analogy: Like a RECEPTION DESK in a hotel where guests (programs) request services (room service, checkout) by speaking to the receptionist (system call interface), who then coordinates with hotel staff (kernel).
